meta.title: Chrome extension cost for MV3 (Chrome/Edge/Firefox)
meta.description: Estimate and build a Manifest V3 extension: content scripts, service worker, Storage API, messaging, OAuth, DOM injection, automation, and store publishing with privacy.

hero.h1: chrome extension cost for Chrome, Edge, and Firefox (Manifest V3)
hero.subtitle: MV3 extension build and publish, with automation, OAuth, and minimal permissions across browsers.

pain.title: When an extension stops being simple
bullets: Have you ever gotten stuck moving to Manifest V3 and replacing background pages with a background service worker?
Have you ever worried that broad permissions will fail store review, or hurt trust, even if features work locally?
Have you ever fought flaky DOM injection and page automation while content scripts and background messaging drift out of sync?

benefits.title: MV3 architecture that holds
benefits.desc: Clear separation between content scripts and the background service worker, with resilient messaging and storage patterns you can maintain.

benefits.title: Automation with guardrails
benefits.desc: Page automation on targets like LinkedIn, Gmail, and other web apps using DOM injection and robust selectors, with limits to avoid unsafe behavior.

benefits.title: Permission scope and privacy
benefits.desc: Minimal host permissions, explicit data handling, and a privacy-first approach aligned with Chrome Web Store policies and real user expectations.

scope.title: What the build includes
bullets: Page automation rules per site (LinkedIn, Gmail, etc.)
Chrome Web Store packaging, listing, and submission
Permission scope review and privacy text

useCases.title: LinkedIn workflows
useCases.desc: Assist repetitive steps, extract visible data, and guide outreach tasks while keeping automation rate limits under control.

useCases.title: Gmail productivity
useCases.desc: Add contextual actions and shortcuts in Gmail with safe DOM hooks, user settings, and predictable state syncing.

useCases.title: Internal tools
useCases.desc: Authenticate with OAuth, store per-user settings, and ship a browser tool your team can trust and support.

useCases.title: CRM and sales enablement
useCases.desc: Add buttons and panels inside web CRMs to reduce clicks, standardize notes, and pull context from the current page.

useCases.title: Validation and QA helpers
useCases.desc: Validate forms and page content on specific domains, with per-site rules, reports, and clear failure handling.

options.name: Manifest V3
options.note: Chrome/Edge MV3 structure with policy-friendly defaults and clear extension boundaries.

options.name: Content Scripts
options.note: DOM injection and in-page automation with isolation, safe selectors, and site-specific fallbacks.

options.name: Service Worker
options.note: Background service worker for events, coordination, alarms, and cross-tab orchestration.

options.name: Storage API
options.note: Local/sync persistence with migration strategy, quota awareness, and consistent reads and writes.

options.name: Messaging
options.note: Runtime and tabs messaging with request/response patterns, retries, and explicit error surfaces.

options.name: OAuth
options.note: OAuth flows when required, with token handling designed to reduce exposure and keep permissions tight.

steps.title: Technical brief
steps.desc: You share target sites, flows, and constraints; you get questions and a scoped plan.

steps.title: Estimate and design
steps.desc: Define MV3 permissions, storage, messaging, and OAuth needs to estimate timeline and cost.

steps.title: Build and publish
steps.desc: Implement, test, package, and support Chrome Web Store publishing, including review feedback fixes.

faq.q: What drives chrome extension cost in practice?
faq.a: Cost is mainly driven by scope: target sites, depth of automation, required permissions, OAuth needs, data volume in storage, and how much store review support you want.

faq.q: Do you build with Manifest V3 (MV3)?
faq.a: Yes. The architecture is MV3-first: content scripts for the page, a background service worker for coordination, and messaging between contexts with explicit error handling.

faq.q: How do you decide permission scope?
faq.a: You start from least privilege. Each feature is mapped to specific host permissions and APIs, avoiding broad access that increases review risk and privacy concerns.

faq.q: Can the extension automate sites like LinkedIn or Gmail?
faq.a: Yes, when it is allowed for your use case. Automation uses content scripts, DOM injection, and guarded flows with rate limits and clear stop conditions.

faq.q: Will you handle Chrome Web Store publishing?
faq.a: Yes. You get packaging, listing assets, submission support, and updates to permissions and privacy disclosures if reviewers request changes.

faq.q: Does it also work on Edge and Firefox?
faq.a: Edge typically follows Chrome closely. Firefox support depends on API compatibility and policy differences, so you get a portability assessment and an adaptation plan when needed.

ctaFinal.title: Get a clear estimate with scope, permissions, and timelines
ctaFinal.desc: Tell me the target pages, whether you need OAuth, and what the automation should do. You get a scoped plan and a quote you can act on.
